entries.leg: Goodbye weekend · South Fork of the Snake (Conant) — LAST fishing day · "Renegade Day"
locations: South Fork of the Snake — Conant boat launch (near South Fork Lodge); hiked down to the island just below — the log-jam outer riffle, the undercut side channel behind the island, and the huge riffle up to its top
entries.summary: The last day, and the best dry-fly day by numbers I've ever had — forever "Renegade Day." I went to the Conant boat launch near South Fork Lodge, parked, and hiked down to the island just below. First fish was a cutthroat on the Renegade near the log-jam outer riffle, and I thought, wow, this could be a great call — come fish this island area and big riffle. I worked the undercut bank behind the island and hooked what I'd put at a 16" cutthroat that eventually broke off in that side channel. Then I started working my way up that huge riffle on the Renegade and just kept catching fish — 24 landed, up and down the riffle. One was an absolute highlight I'll never forget: at the very top of the riffle I spotted a bigger fish active near me; with a beer in my left hand and the rod in my right, I flicked the Renegade twice and on the second flick that sighted fish ate. I set the hook and, with no net and no intention of losing it, worked it all the way down to the head of the gravel bar — so glad I did, because it was a beautiful 16" Snake River fine-spotted cutthroat, and I got a great picture of it in the gravel. Another fish, about 15", was a cutbow I caught in the riffle at the bottom of the island; the rest were 10–14" cutthroats and a rainbow. Every fish but the one that broke off in the side channel ate the Renegade.

catches.notes: 16" — THE fish, and a trip highlight. Sighted active at the very top of the riffle; beer in the left hand, rod in the right, flicked the Renegade twice and he ate on the second. No net, so I worked him all the way down to the head of the gravel bar to land him safely. A gorgeous Snake River fine-spotted cutthroat — got a great photo of it in the gravel.
~15" — in the riffle section at the bottom of the island, on the Renegade.
Up in the big riffle, on the Renegade.
10–14" — up and down the huge riffle (including the first fish near the log-jam outer riffle), all on the Renegade. 24 landed total on the day.

flies: Renegade (classic old attractor dry) — every landed fish (24) ate it; the only fish lost, a ~16" cutthroat that broke off in the side-channel undercut, was the lone one not on the Renegade
entries.notes: "Renegade Day" — the last fishing day of the trip and the best dry-fly day by numbers of my life: 24 landed on a single fly, all up and down one big island riffle on the South Fork below Conant. The standout was the sighted 16" fine-spotted cutthroat I called my shot on (two flicks, beer in hand) and walked down to the gravel bar to land without a net — photographed in the gravel. A ~16" cutthroat broke off earlier in the undercut side channel behind the island (the only fish not on the Renegade, and the only one lost). After Thursday broke the cold run, the Renegade closed the trip out completely.
lessons: Find one piece of structure and mine it. 24 fish came up and down a single big island riffle — once the first cutt ate near the log-jam outer riffle, I committed to that water instead of roaming, and it became the best dry-fly day of my life by numbers.
Sight-fish the eat when you can. The fish of the day was one I spotted active at the top of the riffle, then dropped the Renegade right on him — a targeted, visible eat beats blind casting.

No net? Play it down to the gravel. Without a net I walked the 16" cutthroat all the way down to the head of the gravel bar to land it safely rather than risk it at my feet — patience landed the trip's best fish.
When a fly is this on, ride it all day. After breaking the cold run Thursday, the Renegade took every one of the 24 landed fish — full confidence in a single producer carried two days running.
The only fish lost was the only one not on the Renegade — the ~16" that broke off in the side-channel undercut. A small reinforcement to stick with the pattern that's working.

conditions.air: Swan Valley / South Fork: high ~79°F / low ~54°F.
conditions.waterTemp: South Fork of the Snake — dam-controlled tailwater, no temp sensor, est. ~52–56°F.
conditions.flowCfs: South Fork of the Snake nr Heise ~13,300 cfs (USGS 13037500) — the high end of a week of rising dam-controlled flow.
conditions.weather: Overcast — ~86% cloud mean. Wind max ~14 mph, gusts to ~34. [Open-Meteo]
conditions.hatch: No hatch to match — the fish ate the Renegade attractor up and down the riffle all day. The only fish lost (side-channel undercut) was on a different fly.

entries.leg: Goodbye weekend · Harriman Ranch (AM) + South Fork float with Rob (PM)
locations: Henry's Fork — Harriman (Railroad) Ranch, the riffle into the deeper run just below Millionaire's Pool (wade, late morning into early afternoon)
South Fork of the Snake — Spring Creek boat launch, afternoon float with Rob
entries.summary: The fly that saved the trip. Late morning into early afternoon I waded the Harriman (Railroad) Ranch and tied on a Renegade for the very first time. Right away a big eat, then a missed second fish out of the riffle into the deeper run just below Millionaire's Pool, plus a big rainbow that ate in the main current and another missed eat. Even missing them, the Renegade was drawing fish and I started to become a believer. Rob called and told me to meet him at the Spring Creek boat launch, so I got there around 4pm and we floated. The salmonfly bite was off — no salmonflies at all, which seemed strange since I'd seen them in that same stretch just a few days before. But I caught 3 cutthroat, all around 12", in the riffle on the Renegade and went all-in on a fly I'd never fished before. Rob said it's a very old pattern and was surprised someone had sold it to me. Those 3 cutts were my first fish since Monday the 22nd — a brutal cold run (skunked on the Ranch Tuesday evening, skunked on the Madison Wednesday) finally broken. The Renegade changed everything.

catches.notes: ~12" each — in the riffle on the South Fork float (Spring Creek launch), all on the Renegade. First fish since Monday Jun 22 — the catch that broke a three-day cold run.

flies: Renegade (classic old attractor dry) — first time fishing it; drew a big eat and a missed rainbow at the Ranch, then landed the 3 South Fork cutthroat
entries.notes: The Renegade was the whole story — a fly I'd never fished. Rob (who ran the float) said it's a very old pattern and was surprised a shop sold it to me. It drew a big eat and a missed rainbow at the Ranch in the morning, then put 3 cutthroat in the net on the South Fork float, breaking a cold run that ran back to Monday Jun 22 (two straight skunks: Tue Ranch + Wed Madison). Salmonflies were absent on the float even though they'd been in that stretch days earlier.
lessons: An unfashionable classic can out-fish the 'right' fly. The Renegade — a very old attractor I'd never tried — turned a brutal cold run around. Carry a few old-school attractors and actually fish them.

A hatch can vanish fast on water that just had it. No salmonflies on the float despite seeing them in the same area days before — don't assume last week's bugs are still on; read the day in front of you.
Commit once a fly proves itself. After repeated eats at the Ranch I went all-in on the Renegade and it landed the South Fork cutts — confidence on a producing fly matters.
Cold runs end — keep fishing. Three days without a fish (Mon to Thu) flipped in a single riffle. Grinding through the skunks kept me on the water for the turn.

conditions.air: Island Park (Ranch, AM): high ~73°F / low ~45°F. Swan Valley (South Fork float, PM): high ~77°F / low ~54°F.
conditions.waterTemp: Henry's Fork Ranch — no reach gauge, rich tailwater, est. ~58–62°F. South Fork of the Snake — dam-controlled tailwater, no temp sensor, est. ~52–56°F.
conditions.flowCfs: Henry's Fork nr Island Park (Ranch reach) ~1,250 cfs (USGS 13042500). South Fork of the Snake nr Heise ~12,600 cfs (USGS 13037500) — rising through the week.
conditions.weather: Ranch AM ~54% cloud mean, wind max ~10 mph, gusts to ~23. South Fork PM ~44% cloud mean, wind max ~10 mph, gusts to ~27. [Open-Meteo]
conditions.hatch: Salmonfly bite OFF on the South Fork float — no salmonfly adults seen, despite them being in the same stretch a few days earlier. Fish ate the Renegade attractor, not a hatch-match.

locations: Upper Madison — Lyons Bridge (between the lakes); the heavy riffle/current just below the bridge
entries.summary: Drove over to chase the Madison salmonfly hatch and fished Lyons Bridge, between the lakes, with only a big golden stone all day. Turns out the worker at the Slide Inn should have sent me further down river — up at Lyons Bridge I saw only a couple of salmonflies. I was dead-set on a big fish, so I stuck with the big golden stone the whole time. I missed a couple of good eats but ended up skunked. The biggest look came from a big brown in the heavy riffle current down below the bridge. Then I drove to the Trout Hunter Lodge in Island Park, where I was staying the night, and crashed.
flies: Big golden stone dry (all day — committed to it for a big fish)
entries.notes: A salmonfly chase that didn't pan out — the bugs weren't really at Lyons Bridge (only a couple seen); the Slide Inn worker should have pointed me further downriver. Stayed the night at the Trout Hunter Lodge, Island Park.
lessons: Pin the fly shop down on WHERE, not just whether. The Slide Inn sent me to Lyons Bridge but the salmonflies were further downriver — ask exactly which access/reach the bugs are on before committing the day.

Don't force the big-fish fly when the bugs aren't there. Sticking with one big golden stone all day for a trophy, with only a couple salmonflies around, got me a skunk and a couple of missed eats — match the day, not the dream.
Convert the few eats you get on a tough day. With only a handful of looks, missing a couple of good eats was the whole difference between a skunk and a fish — stay sharp when chances are scarce.
The big riffle current below Lyons Bridge held the better fish (a big brown looked) — note it for a future salmonfly day when the bugs are actually on.

conditions.waterTemp: Upper Madison at Kirby Ranch (Lyons Bridge reach) ~51–65°F, mean ~57°F (USGS 00010).
conditions.flowCfs: Upper Madison at Kirby Ranch (Lyons Bridge) ~931 cfs (USGS 06038800).
conditions.weather: ~49% cloud mean. Light wind — max ~7 mph, gusts to ~19. [Open-Meteo]
conditions.hatch: Salmonfly chase — only a couple of salmonfly adults seen at Lyons Bridge; the hatch was further downriver. Fished a big golden stone; a couple eats missed, a big brown looked in the riffle below the bridge.

locations: Henry's Fork — Harriman (Railroad) Ranch (evening wade)
entries.summary: Evening session on the Harriman (Railroad) Ranch and got skunked — the start of a cold run. Nothing for me here tonight; I wouldn't land another fish until the Renegade turned it around on Thursday's float with Rob.
entries.notes: Skunked on the Ranch in the evening. The first of two straight blanks (this, then the Madison on Wednesday) — my last fish had been Monday Jun 22 on the South Fork, and I wouldn't catch again until Thursday's South Fork float with Rob.
lessons: The Harriman Ranch is technical, demanding water — an evening drop-in without dialing the right hatch/pattern can easily blank. Treat the Ranch as a plan-ahead session, not a quick after-work stop.
One skunk isn't a slump, but log it. This was night one of a multi-day cold run; tracking it kept me from blaming the wrong thing and pushed me to change it up (a new fly) when the run finally broke.

conditions.waterTemp: Henry's Fork Ranch — no reach gauge; rich, slow tailwater/spring water, est. ~58–62°F.
conditions.flowCfs: Henry's Fork nr Island Park (Ranch reach) ~1,240 cfs (USGS 13042500); ~1,100 at St. Anthony.
conditions.weather: Bright — ~8% cloud mean. Wind max ~11 mph, gusts to ~22. [Open-Meteo]
conditions.hatch: Nothing moved for me this evening (skunked); no hatch logged.

entries.leg: Weekend 3 · South Fork of the Snake (wade)
locations: South Fork of the Snake — Fall Creek Falls / island wade water (from South Fork Lodge)
entries.summary: Took Sunday (Jun 21) off as a rest day, then went out Monday on the South Fork. Arrived at South Fork Lodge around 11; they pointed me upriver to Fall Creek Falls for a wade area around the islands. I scaled down to the water on the rope and walked up the entire channel — saw no fish activity. Continued upriver along the road and decided to start fishing at a kayak launch. Surprisingly caught a 14" cutthroat on a stonefly, then missed a bigger fish tight to the bank just downriver. I worked downstream, wading and walking the road, and found a nice small island spot with a big riffle and a good-looking channel. Caught another cutthroat on the stonefly in the riffle where it dumps into the deeper water in a bucket. After those first two on the stonefly I switched down to smaller flies: behind the island, at a point where the channel converges, I caught 5 cutthroats all 10–12" on PMDs and caddis. On the final stretch back to the car I picked up 2 browns on a caddis emerger, just dangling the fly downstream. After I got to the car and started driving there were a ton of stoneflies, so I pulled over and fished one last time — no eats. Went home with a solid day, just no bigger fish.

catches.notes: 14" — on a stonefly at the kayak launch. Then missed a bigger fish tight to the bank just downriver.
On the stonefly — in the riffle at the small island, where it meets the deeper water in a bucket. After this I switched down to the smaller flies.
10–12" — behind the island at the point where the channel converges, on PMDs and caddis.
On the walk back to the car — caddis emerger just dangled/swung downstream.

entries.notes: Solid numbers day (9 landed) but no size. Key observation: despite salmonfly/stonefly activity through the afternoon into evening, the stonefly only produced the first two cutthroat (the kayak-launch fish and the riffle fish) before it went quiet in my wading spots — after that the fish ate PMDs and caddis instead. A guide from a boat said they WERE getting eats, but out on the main river, not the side channels I could wade. The bugs were everywhere (tons of stoneflies on the drive home) but the players were on water I couldn't reach on foot.
lessons: When salmonflies are on big water like the South Fork, the eating fish may be out in the main river where only a boat reaches — wading the side channels/islands got me numbers of small cutts but not the salmonfly eats. A float is the move when the hatch is on here.

Fish what's actually producing, not the hatch you want. The stonefly drew the first two cutts then went quiet; switching down to PMDs and caddis put 5 more in the net behind the island. Match the bugs the fish are eating, not the biggest bug in the air.
Read transition seams. Both the riffle-into-bucket and the channel-convergence point behind the island held fish — target where fast water meets slow and where channels rejoin.
A dangled/swung caddis emerger on the walk out is a free searching tactic — it picked up 2 browns with almost no effort.
Confirm with the boats. The guide's intel (eats on the main river) explained my slow wade and tells me how to play the South Fork salmonfly next time.
Numbers without size is still a good day — 9 fish on a tough wade beats forcing the big-fish water that wasn't accessible.

conditions.waterTemp: South Fork of the Snake nr Heise has no temp sensor; dam-controlled tailwater, est. ~52–56°F.
conditions.flowCfs: Snake River nr Heise (South Fork) 11,400 cfs (USGS 13037500) — high dam-controlled summer flow; Fall Creek Falls reach is below Heise.
conditions.weather: Bright — 7% cloud mean (the bright wade water that didn't produce salmonfly eats). Wind max ~12 mph, gusts to ~29 mph. Salmonflies thick by afternoon. [Open-Meteo]
conditions.hatch: Salmonfly/stonefly active afternoon into evening (bugs everywhere) — but in the wadeable side channels the fish ate PMDs and caddis, not the stonefly. A boat guide reported salmonfly eats out on the main river. Confirmed on the water.

entries.leg: Weekend 3 · South Fork of the Snake (Heise) with Rob & Mike
locations: South Fork of the Snake — Heise reach, salmonfly float (guided by Rob; tip from his friend Chris)
entries.summary: PRELIMINARY LOG — full write-up to come. After two brutally slow days on the lower Henry's Fork, on our Friday float we got a message that Rob would take us on a Snake River salmonfly float — his friend Chris said it was lights out. It goes down as the best fishing experience I've probably ever had in my life. The salmonflies were on and the eats were spectacular. I got into an epic 18" cutthroat — fat, and Rob said about as good a fish as you can get on a dry. Also landed a 16" cutthroat with spectacular spots and color, a 16" brown, and a 16" rainbow, plus some smaller cutties off a big gravel bar (those came on Rob's rod with his PMD). Everything else ate salmonflies. 10 fish landed total and a big rainbow broke me off.

catches.notes: 18" — fat. THE fish: an epic dry-fly eat on a salmonfly. Rob said about as good as you can get on a dry. Highlight of the trip so far.
16" — spectacular spots and color, on a salmonfly.
16" — on a salmonfly.
16" — on a salmonfly.
Off a big gravel bar — caught on Rob's rod with his PMD.

entries.notes: Best fishing day of my life. The Snake salmonfly was genuinely on — confirmed on the water, not hype. Big dry-fly cutthroat eats, and a big rainbow broke off. Rob's local intel (via Chris) completely turned the trip around after the dead lower-river floats. Full details and conditions to be added in the next log.
lessons: Chase the water that's actually fishing. Two dead days on the lower Henry's Fork flipped to the best day of my life the moment we moved to the Snake where the salmonfly was on — mobility and local intel beat grinding dead water.
Trust the network. Rob's tip from Chris ('lights out') was the whole difference — when a trusted local says a hatch is on somewhere, go.
When the salmonfly is genuinely on, the big fish come up for the dry — the 18" cutthroat and a string of 16-inchers all ate on top.
Keep a PMD ready as the change-up — the gravel-bar cutties came on the PMD when the big-dry bite wasn't the play in that spot.
Pressure on light tippet with big fish: a big rainbow broke off — for fish this size on dries, check the tippet/knots and don't over-horse.

conditions.air: Daily high ~72°F / low ~53–55°F — a wet, cloudy, cool-ish day across the region.
conditions.waterTemp: South Fork of the Snake nr Heise — dam-controlled tailwater, no temp sensor, est. ~52–56°F.
conditions.flowCfs: South Fork of the Snake nr Heise ~11,400 cfs (USGS 13037500) — high dam-controlled summer flow; the float was on this stretch (Heise reach).
conditions.weather: Heavy overcast (~63–66% cloud mean) with showers around — wind max ~10–14 mph, gusts to ~28–36 mph. The cloud cover made prime salmonfly dry-fly conditions. [Open-Meteo]
conditions.hatch: Salmonfly ON — confirmed on the water (Karel fished it), tipped by Rob's friend Chris as 'lights out.' Big dry-fly eats; a PMD produced smaller cutthroat off a gravel bar.

entries.leg: Weekend 3 · Henry's Fork float with Mike
locations: Lower Henry's Fork — Ora to Chester (drift)
entries.summary: Second float day with Mike in Rob's borrowed driftboat, Ora down to Chester. Not a single eat or hookup for me all day. I stuck with stoneflies the entire day again — I've been wanting to get reps with them and pull up the bigger fish — but the river gave nothing. We pulled the boat out extremely disappointed. After two days floating the lower river, this was probably the least amount of fish and bug activity we've seen this time of year in 9 years of fishing this trip together. The one bright spot came on the float: a message that Rob would take us on a Snake River salmonfly float the next day — his friend Chris said it was lights out.
entries.notes: A full skunk on the Ora–Chester float. Stuck deliberately with stoneflies to practice and target size. Lowest fish/bug activity we've seen on this stretch this time of year in 9 years. Saved by the news of a Snake River salmonfly float for Saturday.
lessons: Know when the water is just off. Committing to stoneflies to practice is fine, but a zero-eat day with almost no bug activity meant the lower river was dead — no fly was going to fix it.
Two slow days on the same water is a signal to change rivers, not flies. The lower Henry's Fork was unusually quiet; the answer turned out to be moving to the Snake.
Deliberate practice has a cost on slow days — all-day stoneflies for reps and size meant passing up easier numbers; worth it only if size is genuinely the goal.
Stay plugged into local intel even mid-float — the Snake salmonfly tip that saved the trip came in while we were on the water.

conditions.flowCfs: Lower Henry's Fork ~1,150 cfs at St. Anthony (USGS 13050500) / 1,300 nr Rexburg (13056500). Ora–Chester reach.
conditions.weather: Hot, ~38% cloud mean. Light wind — max ~9 mph, gusts to ~18 mph. [Open-Meteo]
conditions.hatch: Almost none — the standout was the near-total LACK of bug activity; lowest we've seen on this stretch this time of year in 9 years. No eats on stoneflies all day.

entries.leg: Weekend 3 · Henry's Fork float with Mike
locations: Lower Henry's Fork — Ora to Vernon (drift)
entries.summary: After staying the night in Jackson we picked up Mike's rental truck and met Rob to grab his driftboat in Victor (Rob lent us the boat — he wasn't fishing with us). First float of the weekend: Mike and I ran Ora down to Vernon. I caught one 16" brown on a stonefly and one very small brown. Fishing was very slow overall. I did miss a nice brown trout eat on a perfect drift against a rock — the kind you don't want to miss.

catches.notes: 16" — on a stonefly dry.
Very small brown.

entries.notes: Met Rob, picked up his driftboat in Victor, floated Ora–Vernon with Mike. Slow day. Missed a nice brown on a perfect drift tight to a rock.
lessons: Be ready on the perfect drift. The one good fish of the day — a nice brown against a rock — ate a clean drift and I missed the set. On slow days the few quality eats are precious; stay sharp on the bank targets.
Bank rocks hold the better browns. The quality eat came tight to a rock; prioritize tucking the fly to structure when the open water is dead.
Slow float days still produce if you grind the right water — one solid 16" brown came on the stonefly even when activity was low.

conditions.waterTemp: No active gauge on this reach; est. ~58–62°F.
conditions.flowCfs: Lower Henry's Fork ~1,140 cfs at St. Anthony (USGS 13050500) / 1,250 nr Rexburg (13056500). Ora–Vernon reach.
conditions.weather: Mostly clear (35% cloud mean). Light wind — max ~9 mph, gusts to ~17 mph. [Open-Meteo]
conditions.hatch: Very slow — little bug activity. A stonefly dry produced the 16" brown.

locations: Lower Henry's Fork — above Vernon Bridge, river left (wade)
entries.summary: Fished much of the day at Vernon until I needed to drive to Jackson to pick up Mike from the airport. I worked upriver on river left from Vernon, fishing the bank. Missed around 4 eats on the stonefly and didn't hook any. Just above Vernon Bridge on river left I worked a brown — he missed it once, and the second time I got a refusal. Then I drove to Jackson and stayed the night.
entries.notes: A frustrating wade day before the drive to Jackson to pick up Mike — about 4 eats missed on the stonefly, no hookups, plus a miss-then-refusal from a brown just above Vernon Bridge (river left).
lessons: Fix the hookup problem on the big stonefly. Four missed eats and no hookups in a day points to timing (setting too early/late) or the fly — consider a slight hesitation on the set, a smaller stone, or a trailing dropper to convert eats.
A refusal after a miss means the fish saw something off — drag, the size, or me. On a second look, change the angle or downsize rather than showing the same drift.
River-left bank above Vernon Bridge holds browns worth targeting — note it for next time.
Plan fishing around hard stops. With a Jackson airport pickup looming, the day was cut short — front-load the best water when the clock is running.

conditions.flowCfs: 1,250 cfs — Henry's Fork nr Rexburg (USGS 13056500); 1,160 at St. Anthony.
conditions.weather: Bluebird — 1% cloud mean. Wind max ~16 mph, gusts to ~33 mph; eased toward dusk. [Open-Meteo]
conditions.hatch: Slow — fished a stonefly dry on the bank; ~4 eats missed, a brown refused above Vernon Bridge. No reliable hatch to key on.

locations: Lower Henry's Fork — above the Vernon boat ramp
entries.summary: An amazing session late afternoon into early evening above the Vernon boat ramp. High winds were blowing stoneflies onto the water and the fish were keyed on them. Everything was on a Mike Lawson–style smaller stonefly dry. I hooked several, missed a couple, and landed 2 browns — one a powerful, thick 16-incher. The eats came both out in the main current and in the seams a bit closer to the bank. Then it all shut off. A memorable evening because I could watch the wind put the bugs on the water and the fish respond. Later, late at night in the moonlight, there were lots of risers working at the boat ramp again — as there always is.

catches.notes: 16" — powerful and thick. On a Mike Lawson–style small stonefly dry; ate out in the main current.
Second brown on the same stonefly dry; also hooked several others and missed a couple this session.

entries.notes: Wind was the trigger, not the obstacle — it blew stoneflies onto the water and the fish ate hard in both the main current and the bank seams. Then the bite shut off. Notable: Sunday through tonight the reliable late-evening caddis hatch produced zero rises/activity — odd, since this water has had a strong evening caddis for the last 10 years. Yet late tonight under the moon the boat-ramp pod was up and feeding as it always does — feels like a big drop-off in the usual evening caddis.
lessons: Wind can be the trigger, not the enemy — the high wind blew stoneflies onto the water and turned the fish on. When it's howling, look at what it's delivering to the surface and match it.
Don't only fish the edges. The eats came in the main current as well as the bank seams — when bugs are on the whole river, cover the heavy water too.
Sessions have a window; it 'all shut off' after the flurry. Fish hard while the bite is on and don't expect it to last.

Pay attention to a missing hatch. Three evenings with no caddis activity on water that's been reliable for a decade is a signal, not noise — log it and adjust expectations (the late-night moonlight risers are a separate, later window).

conditions.waterTemp: No active gauge (Rexburg offline); est. ~58–62°F evening.
conditions.flowCfs: 1,240 cfs — Henry's Fork nr Rexburg (USGS 13056500); 1,180 at St. Anthony.
conditions.weather: Hot, ~41% cloud mean. HIGH WIND — wind max ~21 mph, gusts to ~39 mph — the wind that blew stoneflies onto the water (confirmed by gauge). [Open-Meteo]
conditions.hatch: Stoneflies blown onto the water by the wind drove the eats on a small stonefly dry. The usual late-evening caddis hatch produced NO rises/activity (notably absent). Late-night moonlight risers at the boat ramp as always.

locations: Lower Henry's Fork — downstream of Bill's Hole
entries.summary: Back home, fished downstream of Bill's Hole on the lower Henry's Fork and got skunked. The expected late-evening caddis hatch never showed — no rises, no activity — same as Sunday evening.
entries.notes: A blank night. Part of an odd stretch (Sun–Tue) where the reliable late-evening caddis hatch simply hasn't materialized on water that's had it for years.
lessons: Even home water blanks. Working new water downstream of the usual Bill's Hole didn't produce — sometimes the known hole holds fish for a reason, and exploring on a dead night just confirms it.

Notice the pattern forming: a second straight evening with no caddis activity. When a dependable hatch goes missing two or three nights running, stop waiting on it and fish searching patterns / attractors instead.
A skunk on a short after-work session isn't worth grinding — bank the rest and save it for a night the conditions turn (which Tuesday's wind did).

conditions.air: Daily high 79°F / low 44°F — hot, breezy evening.
conditions.flowCfs: 1,270 cfs — Henry's Fork nr Rexburg (USGS 13056500); 1,180 at St. Anthony.
conditions.weather: Partly cloudy (40% cloud mean). Wind max ~8 mph, gusts to ~17 mph. [Open-Meteo]
conditions.hatch: No late-evening caddis activity — the expected hatch never came off (notably absent, same as Sunday).

locations: Valley Garden — Madison below Ennis
Lyons Bridge — upper Madison (between the lakes)
Bill's Hole — lower Henry's Fork (home)
entries.summary: Drove home from the Big Hole by way of the Madison. Started at Valley Garden below Ennis and got skunked — no salmonfly adults around, which matched the dated Jun 7–10 reports that had the hatch only just approaching, not up. Rather than write the day off, I gave it another try to end the evening up at Lyons Bridge, between the lakes. About 100 yards upstream I got 2 browns on a caddis dry. Then I continued home and hit Bill's Hole on the way in — nothing there.

catches.notes: Lyons Bridge, ~100 yards upstream — both on a caddis dry to end the evening.

entries.notes: Valley Garden was dead — no salmonfly adults, exactly as the FlyFishFood (Jun 7) / River's Edge (Jun 10) reports had it. Moving up to Lyons Bridge and fishing a simple caddis dry salvaged the evening. Bill's Hole gave up nothing on the way home — the start of three evenings with no caddis action there.
lessons: The dated reports were right — no salmonfly adults at Valley Garden. Don't drive to the Madison banking on a hatch the reports haven't confirmed; treat adults as a bonus, not the plan.
Move to find willing fish. Valley Garden was a skunk, but driving up to Lyons Bridge and working ~100 yards turned 2 browns — fresh water beat grinding dead water.
A simple caddis dry out-produced chasing the unconfirmed salmonfly. Fish what the river is actually giving you.
Stacking one more stop at the end of a travel day paid off — the Lyons Bridge browns came after I'd already been skunked and could have gone straight home.

conditions.air: Daily high 64°F / low 40°F (Madison/Ennis); warmer ~72°F / 50°F back home on the lower Henry's Fork.
conditions.waterTemp: Madison below Ennis Lake (Valley Garden) 58.5°F; upper Madison between the lakes at Kirby Ranch (Lyons Bridge reach) 53.6°F (USGS 00010).
conditions.flowCfs: Madison below Ennis Lake (Valley Garden) 1,530 cfs (USGS 06041000); upper Madison at Kirby Ranch (Lyons Bridge) 1,240 cfs (USGS 06038800); Bill's Hole / lower Henry's Fork 1,120 cfs (USGS 13056500).
conditions.weather: Partly cloudy (27% cloud mean on the Madison). Wind max ~9 mph, gusts to ~21 mph. Warm with some cloud on the drift home. [Open-Meteo]
conditions.hatch: No salmonfly adults at Valley Garden (matching the Jun 7–10 reports — not up). A caddis dry drew 2 browns at Lyons Bridge. No caddis activity later at Bill's Hole.

locations: Poindexter Slough — spring creek near Dillon
entries.summary: Bought a new rod and setup from Frontier Anglers in Dillon — the shop pointed me to the Poindexter access and gave me fly tips. Arrived at the creek around 11am. The sun came out and a strong PMD hatch came off with all kinds of trout rising in the first hole. Put on a small PMD and caught about 8 trout from 10–15" without ever changing holes — technical fishing, drifting the fly into undercuts and tough spots. Eventually moved up to the next bend and saw a couple of nice trout feeding in the head of the pool in the riffle. Took a medium one first, then a couple more moved up to feed in the same lane; I didn't get the biggest to eat but landed a nice 15–16-incher. Took lots of breaks — another memorable session.

catches.notes: First hole on a small PMD, 10–15", drifted into undercuts — never changed holes
Medium fish, head of the pool in the riffle at the next bend
Nice 15–16" in the riffle-head feeding lane

entries.notes: New rod and setup from Frontier Anglers, Dillon — they tipped me off to Poindexter. Spring-creek fishing: clear water, technical drifts, feeding fish in defined lanes.
lessons: Local shop intel pays off — Frontier Anglers put me on Poindexter and the right fly with one stop.
When a hatch is on and fish are stacked, work the one hole thoroughly instead of moving — 8 fish came from a single hole.
On clear spring creeks the eats are in the undercuts; getting a clean drift tight to the bank is what unlocked the tough spots.
Riffle heads pull feeders up into defined lanes — read the lane, drift it, and more fish keep moving up into the same spot.

conditions.air: Daily high 63°F / low 41°F (Dillon). Lived: 57°F at 11am rising to ~64°F mid-afternoon.
conditions.waterTemp: Poindexter is a spring creek (no gauge) — spring-fed, runs cold and stable; est. ~50–54°F.
conditions.flowCfs: No gauge on Poindexter Slough (small spring creek). Regional context: Beaverhead River at Barretts ~547 cfs (USGS 06016000).
conditions.weather: Mixed sun and cloud (~30% cloud mean; clouded up through the afternoon as observed). Wind max ~13 mph, gusts to ~27 mph. [Open-Meteo]
conditions.hatch: Strong PMD hatch once the sun came out

locations: Fish Trap access — upper Big Hole
Powerhouse access — Big Hole
entries.summary: Drove to the Fish Trap access on the upper Big Hole. Caught 3 brook trout, 1 rainbow, 1 grayling, and 1 whitefish — the grayling was the real highlight, and it ate a golden stone dry. Didn't spot any big fish, but it was nice to see these upper waters. On the way back I went looking for bigger water and stopped at the Powerhouse access. Walked under the bridge and worked slowly downstream — immediately caught a small brown on the golden stone dry. Saw a split in the river with an island forming, followed it up, and in the first hole a trout attacked the golden stone. From there it was one after another, all violent eats. The run turned out to be a diversion channel. In about 90 minutes I caught at least 10 trout from 12–15", almost all brown and a bit sleek and thin. The key: skating the golden stone on the swing triggered hit after hit — I've never seen takes like that on a swung golden stone dry. Saw one bigger fish come look but that was the closest I got. A memorable session I'll never forget.

catches.notes: Fish Trap access, golden stone dry
Fish Trap access
Highlight of the day — ate a golden stone dry at Fish Trap
Fish Trap access
Powerhouse diversion channel, 12–15", sleek and thin — violent eats on a SKATED golden stone dry swung across the current

entries.notes: Two very different stretches: upper Big Hole at Fish Trap for species variety, then the Powerhouse diversion channel for a stacked run of aggressive browns. The diversion channel was the find of the day. No salmonfly hatch going — Sunrise Fly Shop said the Big Hole salmonflies were long gone this year and it had been a short hatch; the fish ate a golden stone dry, not salmonflies.
lessons: Skating/swinging the golden stone dry across the current — not a dead drift — triggered violent hit-after-hit eats. New go-to trigger when fish are aggressive.
Scouting for bigger water paid off — leaving Fish Trap to check the Powerhouse access is what found the hot run.
Diversion and side channels can stack aggressive fish; the split/island that looked like a dead end was the best water of the day.
Upper Big Hole gives species variety (brookies, grayling, whitefish) but smaller fish — drop downstream for size.

Don't trust calendar/seasonal salmonfly hype — Sunrise Fly Shop confirmed the Big Hole salmonfly was long gone and short this year. The golden stone carried the day. Chasing the salmonfly proper, head to the Madison.

conditions.air: Daily high 73°F / low 42°F (Melrose). Lived: 56°F at 10am → ~64°F early afternoon → 54°F by evening.
conditions.waterTemp: Lower Big Hole nr Melrose 57.2°F; upper Big Hole at Wisdom (Fish Trap reach) 58.5°F (USGS 00010, daily).
conditions.flowCfs: Big Hole nr Melrose (Powerhouse reach) 1,430 cfs (USGS 06025500); upper Big Hole at Wisdom (Fish Trap) 52.5 cfs (USGS 06024450).
conditions.weather: Mostly clear (21% cloud mean). Warm and windy — wind max ~13 mph, gusts to ~39 mph. [Open-Meteo]
conditions.hatch: No salmonfly (long gone this year per Sunrise Fly Shop — a short hatch); fish took a golden stone dry

locations: Bill's Hole — lower Henry's Fork
entries.summary: Arrived at Bill's Hole about 6pm and fished until 9pm. Ran through several flies early with no luck — I think BWO and caddis. Then switched to a golden stone foam, cast it into the head of the riffle, and let it float down into the hole. Amazed to see a big brown turn in the riffle, follow the stone down, and slam it — my best eat of the trip so far because I watched it all happen. Landed it by banking it: a thick 16-incher. Switched to a couple of two-fly setups after that and only landed a 6" brown on a spinner tied off the back of the lead fly. It cooled down and the rises shut down rather than building into a big caddis hatch, so I left content and a little earlier than previous nights.

catches.notes: 16" — thick. Golden stone foam cast into the head of the riffle and drifted into the hole; watched a big brown turn, follow, and slam it. Best eat of the trip. Banked it.
6" — on a spinner tied off the back of the lead fly in a two-fly setup.

entries.notes: Best eat of the trip — watched the whole thing happen: the golden stone foam drifted off the riffle head and a big brown turned, followed it down, and crushed it. Banked a thick 16". Now I've pulled a big rainbow and a nice brown out of Bill's Hole. Left a little earlier and content once the rises shut down. Taking a couple nights off to restock, hit the fly shop for floatant and small flies, plan the Thursday–Sunday trip, and rest up / catch up on work.
lessons: When the small stuff fails, swing big. BWO and caddis drew nothing, but a high-floating golden stone foam fished through the head of the riffle and drifted into the hole pulled the best fish of the trip up out of the pool — a 16" brown that followed and slammed it.

Fish where you can see the eat. The reason this was the best take of the trip is I watched it unfold in the riffle — positioning to sight-fish the riffle head beats blind-casting into the dark of the pool.

The two-fly rig only produced the small brown on the spinner dropper while the lead golden stone did the real work — when one fly is clearly carrying the session, weigh the dropper's tangle/drag cost against its payoff.

Read the closing window. No big caddis blizzard this night — the cooling shut the rises down instead of building a hatch. Leaving content rather than grinding a dead pool preserved energy and morale for the Thursday–Sunday trip.

conditions.air: Daily high 70°F / low 44°F. Lived: cooling through the session 70°F at 6pm → 60°F by 9pm.
conditions.flowCfs: 1,630 cfs — Henry's Fork nr Rexburg (USGS 13056500); 1,160 at St. Anthony. Dropping off runoff.
conditions.weather: Fully overcast (81% cloud mean). Light wind — max ~6 mph, gusts to ~14 mph. On the water ~6–9pm. [Open-Meteo]
conditions.hatch: Tried BWO and caddis early with no eats. No big caddis blizzard this night — the cooling shut the rises down rather than building a hatch. A golden stone foam attractor drew the big brown out of the riffle head.

locations: Bill's Hole — lower Henry's Fork (evening)
entries.summary: Inside all day cooking and working, but the wind gusts started to ease so I went for it. Got to Bill's Hole at 8pm and fished until 9:45pm. Tied on a medium attractor with a caddis-emerger type dropper about a foot off the back. No clean takes at first but a couple of tugs, then I came tight to a 12" rainbow on the emerger — again hooked on the bottom side of the mouth, so not sure if it was a partial/uncommitted take or lightly foul-hooked, but I'm counting it. The wind died right down as it got later and the air went cool. Not a big hatch, but some caddis activity, a pod working below me again and some movement at the head of the riffle. Never landed a bigger fish off either pod, but visibility was slightly better than last night. Didn't get skunked.

catches.notes: 12" — on the caddis emerger dropper. Hooked on the underside of the mouth again; possibly an uncommitted take, counting it.

entries.notes: Short, focused evening session that paid off after two slow days. Wind eased through the night; cool air at dark. Then a real scare — couldn't find my car key and started worrying I'd be stuck outside all night, until I found it zipped in a pocket of my fly vest back at the car. Big panic.
lessons: Even in bad wind and behind a cold front, the last couple hours toward dark can fish well as things calm — worth going out late instead of writing the day off.

Have a car-key system. Nearly got stranded; the key was buried in a zipped vest pocket. Pick one dedicated spot for it every time, or leave the car unlocked (with the key hidden in it) so I could sleep in it in a pinch.
The dry-dropper (attractor + caddis emerger ~1 ft back) out-fished the single dry — the emerger drew the eat when nothing committed on top.

Still need to crack these pressured pods. Slightly better visibility tonight; next try stalking closer and shortening the cast rather than reaching from distance — they see a lot of anglers and boats.
Two fish in a row hooked on the underside of the mouth suggests short/late takes — maybe slow the swing, add a touch of hesitation before the set, or downsize the emerger to get cleaner eats.

conditions.air: Daily high 65°F / low 49°F (cold front). Lived: ~63°F at 6pm → 58°F by 8pm → 54°F by 9pm.
conditions.flowCfs: 1,600 cfs — Henry's Fork nr Rexburg (USGS 13056500); 1,210 at St. Anthony.
conditions.weather: Clear behind the front (41% cloud mean). Very windy early — wind max ~24 mph, gusts to ~45 mph — laying down through the evening. On the water ~8–9:45pm. [Open-Meteo]
conditions.hatch: Modest caddis activity after dark, not a heavy hatch. A pod working below and some movement at the head of the riffle.

locations: Lower Henry's Fork — Vernon Bridge down to the rapids
Bill's Hole (evening)
entries.summary: A ten-hour grind on about 3–4 hours of sleep — adrenaline from being here and that big rainbow, plus a new place always taking some adjusting. On the water by 10am. Started just below Vernon Bridge near the two metal farm-feed silos, backpack loaded with beer, Gatorade, and snacks. Not much rising, so I worked down to the big-rock spot above the famous big-bank pool (up above the rapids, the deep run into the duck-pond pool). Nothing on the way down and nothing there. Wind picked up. Pushed down to the big bend that's known for holding big browns on the bank — had a large brown turn and look at the Chubby but he didn't commit. Went just above the rapids, turned, and worked my way back up. Took two spills — one heading down, a worse one coming back up where I cracked my knee on a river rock (lucky it wasn't worse). By then I was wiped. Back to the car around midday-ish to restock, then over to Bill's Hole about 6pm. Caddis started a little but nothing like Friday night. The intense wind finally laid down after about 5pm, temps cooled, and there were stretches of very low light. Hooked into a brown at the head of the pool but it was a surprise and I didn't set — lesson learned. Worked back and forth below and at the head trying to reach feeding pods, but I was too exhausted to see my fly in the low light and couldn't track a caddis well enough to time the eats.

catches.notes: 8" — small, by the big-rock pool on the way back up.

entries.notes: Basically a 10-hour day on the river with very little activity. Wind was the story until ~5pm; after it dropped the light went flat and low. A big brown at the big bend turned on the Chubby but didn't eat. Beers and snacks throughout.
lessons: Rest matters — fishing on 3–4 hours of sleep wrecked my stamina and focus by evening. Get a real night before a full day.
Set on the surprise eat. Lost a brown at the head of Bill's Hole because I wasn't ready — keep tension and be ready to set even when it's slow.
Low-light fly visibility is the limiter. Need a plan: a higher-vis post/indicator on the dry, a lighter sighter, or fishing a known seam by feel so I can time the eat when I can't see the fly.
These Vernon pods see heavy pressure (anglers + boats) and get picky. Feeding line to them below me was a struggle — work on a clean downstream/reach presentation so the fly leads the leader.
Wind = stay home until it lays down. The productive window opened only after ~5pm when it calmed; could've saved energy by starting later or resting through the gusts.

conditions.air: Daily high 81°F / low 52°F. Lived: warm midday ~80°F by 2pm, a cloud bank cooled it to ~73°F by 6pm and 69°F by 8pm.
conditions.flowCfs: 1,630 cfs — Henry's Fork nr Rexburg (USGS 13056500); 1,210 at St. Anthony. Still dropping.
conditions.weather: Clouding up — 60% cloud mean (clear midday building to overcast ~99% by ~4pm). Wind max ~16 mph, gusts to ~32 mph; eased to ~7–8 mph by evening. On the water ~10am–8pm. [Open-Meteo]
conditions.hatch: Very little all day. A weak caddis start at Bill's Hole after ~6pm — nothing like Friday's blizzard. Sporadic pods in the low evening light.

locations: Vernon Bridge / Lower Henry's Fork — Bill's Hole
entries.summary: Drove straight from the airport to Vernon and walked down to Bill's Hole — the same water where last June I floated rose petals down the river for Dad. A lady was already in the hole, so I hung back above her and worked the run. Stuck with a Chubby Chernobyl all evening even though no golden stones showed. Around 8pm, as the sun dropped, the first head appeared against the bank above me — a nice brown. Cast a few times, hung the bush over him, then spooked him walking up to free the fly. When the lady below packed out I dropped into Bill's Hole and fished it for about an hour and a half, right where I wanted to be. A pod started working a heavy caddis hatch; I stayed with the Chubby and after a lot of casts one finally swiped and I came tight.

catches.notes: 18" — hooked on the underside of the mouth, maybe a partial take, but I'm counting it. Forgot my net, so I banked it. Cleaned it after.

entries.notes:
Bill's Hole is where I held the rose-petal ceremony for Dad (d. March 4, 2025) last year — good to be back on that water.

Cast to a brown rising tight to the bank above me — never hooked him, just made a few presentations before moving on. Fished a while longer over the rising pod after landing the rainbow, stopped around 10pm and got home about 10:30. A few beers and American Spirits along the way.
lessons: Bring the net. Forgot it and had to bank an 18" fish — fine this time, but risky for the fish and for landing a good one.
Don't approach a snagged fly when a fish is working above you. Stepping up to free the bush spooked the bank brown — break it off or wait rather than wading toward the lie.
Confidence-fly persistence works. Stuck with the Chubby through a heavy caddis pod and got the eat after many casts — sometimes the fly isn't the problem, the count of good drifts is.

conditions.air: Daily high 79°F / low 45°F. Lived: cooled from 79°F at 6pm → 73°F at 8pm (first riser) → 67°F by 10pm.
conditions.waterTemp: Lower Henry's Fork has no active temp gauge (Rexburg 13056500 sensor offline since 2016); est. ~52–56°F evening.
conditions.flowCfs: 1,770 cfs — Henry's Fork nr Rexburg (USGS 13056500); 1,260 cfs at St. Anthony (13050500). Dropping off runoff.
conditions.weather: Clear (5% cloud mean), no rain. Wind max ~12 mph, gusts to ~24 mph — easing to near calm at dusk (the calm lined up with the caddis pod). On the water ~6–10pm. [weather: Open-Meteo reanalysis]
conditions.hatch: Heavy caddis hatch after ~8pm with a pod feeding hard; no golden stones seen. Chubby Chernobyl drew the eats.
